FT_IC1.evidence: Baseline data from 10,842 participants in the Adolescent Brain Cognitive Development (ABCD) Study enrolled from 21 U.S. study sites between September 2016 and October 2018.

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical cross-sectional analysis using propensity-score matched ABCD data; reports prevalence ratios for screen time categories in association with obesity.

FT_IC6.evidence: Published peer-reviewed article in Frontiers in Pediatrics; no indication of duplicate.

FT_IC2.evidence: Step 1 hit: Table 3 reports a prevalence ratio of 1.42 (95% CI 1.00-2.01, p=0.05) for texting (30+ min/day vs 0 min) in association with obesity, a physical health/individual-trait outcome. Texting is a phone/SM-specific variable with its own coefficient paired with an individual-trait outcome.

FT_IC3.evidence: Prevalence ratios with 95% CIs reported in Tables 3-5 for associations between screen time modalities and obesity.

FT_IC4.evidence: Inferential analysis performed; numeric estimates (prevalence ratios, 95% CIs, p-values) reported throughout Tables 2-5.

FT_EC7_reason: Cross-sectional design (baseline data only); FT-EC7 requires longitudinal design. Condition (a) not met.
notes: Paper examines screen time types (passive viewing, video games, social media/video chat, texting) in relation to autism status and obesity. Key qualifying estimate: texting duration vs obesity prevalence ratio = 1.42. Social media/video chat also reported separately but did not reach significance (p=0.58).

FT_IC1.evidence: Data from the U.S. ABCD Study (ABCD 2.0 release, NDA), 8324 children aged 9-11 from 21 data collection sites across the United States.

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical analysis of ABCD data using resting-state fMRI connectivity and behavioral measures across baseline and 2-year follow-up waves.

FT_IC6.evidence: Published peer-reviewed article in Developmental Cognitive Neuroscience; no indication of duplicate.

FT_IC2.evidence: Step 1: All results (correlations, group comparisons, mediation paths) use the Screen-Activity Proportion (SAP) composite score only; no per-modality breakout estimate appears anywhere in the paper. Step 2: The SAP is derived from a daily screen time sum that includes watching TV shows/movies, watching videos, playing video games, texting, and video chatting — a mix of non-phone/non-SM ingredients (TV/movies, unspecified-platform video games) bundled with phone/SM items, with no separate per-modality coefficient reported. Composite fails Step 2.

notes: The SAP score bundles all screen activities into one ratio; no per-modality (e.g., social media, texting) coefficient is reported in any results table or supplement. The 12-item screen time questionnaire includes TV shows/movies and unspecified-platform video games alongside phone/SM items, making the composite a mixed non-phone/SM measure.

FT_IC1.evidence: Data from the Adolescent Brain Cognitive Development (ABCD) Study, N=11,633 children from 21 diverse U.S. sites, ABCD 4.0 release (baseline 2016-2018).

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical prospective cohort analysis using logistic regression to estimate associations between baseline screen time and suicidal behaviors at 2-year follow-up.

FT_IC6.evidence: Published peer-reviewed article in Preventive Medicine; no indication of duplicate.

FT_IC2.evidence: Step 1 hit: Table 2 reports separate adjusted ORs for texting (aOR 1.36, 95% CI 1.06-1.74, p=0.014) and video chat (aOR 1.30, 95% CI 1.03-1.65, p=0.028) in association with suicidal behaviors (a mental health/individual-trait outcome). Texting and video chat are phone/SM-specific variables with their own coefficients paired with an individual-trait outcome.

FT_IC3.evidence: Adjusted ORs with 95% CIs and p-values reported in Table 2 for each screen time modality including texting and video chat.

FT_IC4.evidence: Logistic regression with quantitative estimates (ORs, 95% CIs) reported for the SM/phone variable to suicidal behaviors relationship.

FT_EC7_reason: Screen time (including texting, video chat, social media) is the IV/exposure in all analyses, and suicidal behaviors is the DV/outcome. FT-EC7 requires SM to be DV in every analysis — here SM is IV throughout. Condition (b) not met.
notes: Prospective cohort (longitudinal) design with baseline screen time predicting 2-year follow-up suicidal behaviors. Social networking aOR=1.27 (p=0.076) was not significant after adjustment, but texting and video chat were significant qualifying estimates.
